coredata.dc:title: Microplasma-induced in situ rapid synthesis of CoSe nanosphere@N-doped polymeric carbon dots derived from ZIF-67 for highly sensitive dopamine detection

coredata.dc:description:
Background Designing a fast and sensitive electrochemical sensing platform to achieve selective quantitative detection of dopamine (DA) is a great challenge. Combining transition metal selenides (TMSs) with a variety of conductive carbonaceous materials is one of the effective strategies to improve the electrocatalytic activity of TMSs. However, most of the reported preparation methods of TMSs/carbon-based composite nanomaterials need to be annealed at a high temperature for a long time, which does not meet the requirements of sustainable development. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore an energy-efficient and fast method to prepare these compounds.
Results In this work, CoSe nanosphere@nitrogen-doped polymeric carbon dots are rapid prepared using ZIF precursor by simple dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma-induced on carbon cloth (CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC) for the first time. Owing to the fact that CoSe can promote rapid proton transfer, N-CDs has a high specific surface area, rich functional groups and electrical conductivity, this electrode exhibits highly sensitive non-enzymatic electrochemical sensing performance for DA detection. The linear range and detection limit are 0.1 μM–50 μM and 40.2 nM, respectively, and it have been successfully applied to the determination of DA levels in real human serum samples. Theoretical DFT calculations show that the most efficient interaction with DA on the surface of CoSe (101) can promote electrochemical reactions and catalyze DA oxidation.
Significance Using ZIF as precursor, CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrochemical electrode was synthesized in situ by simple and energy-saving DBD microplasma. CoSe NSs can effectively prevent the aggregation of function-rich N-PCDs and significantly improve the electrocatalytic activity of the composite. The mechanism of high selectivity of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrode to DA was studied by DFT calculation. This work provides a new idea for the fast and green synthesis of transition metal and carbon-based nanomaterials by microplasma.

head.ce:title: Microplasma-induced in situ rapid synthesis of CoSe nanosphere@N-doped polymeric carbon dots derived from ZIF-67 for highly sensitive dopamine detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing a fast and sensitive electrochemical sensing platform to achieve selective quantitative detection of dopamine (DA) is a great challenge. Combining transition metal selenides (TMSs) with a variety of conductive carbonaceous materials is one of the effective strategies to improve the electrocatalytic activity of TMSs. However, most of the reported preparation methods of TMSs/carbon-based composite nanomaterials need to be annealed at a high temperature for a long time, which does not meet the requirements of sustainable development. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore an energy-efficient and fast method to prepare these compounds.

In this work, CoSe nanosphere@nitrogen-doped polymeric carbon dots are rapid prepared using ZIF precursor by simple dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma-induced on carbon cloth (CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC) for the first time. Owing to the fact that CoSe can promote rapid proton transfer, N-CDs has a high specific surface area, rich functional groups and electrical conductivity, this electrode exhibits highly sensitive non-enzymatic electrochemical sensing performance for DA detection. The linear range and detection limit are 0.1 μM–50 μM and 40.2 nM, respectively, and it have been successfully applied to the determination of DA levels in real human serum samples. Theoretical DFT calculations show that the most efficient interaction with DA on the surface of CoSe (101) can promote electrochemical reactions and catalyze DA oxidation.

Using ZIF as precursor, CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrochemical electrode was synthesized in situ by simple and energy-saving DBD microplasma. CoSe NSs can effectively prevent the aggregation of function-rich N-PCDs and significantly improve the electrocatalytic activity of the composite. The mechanism of high selectivity of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrode to DA was studied by DFT calculation. This work provides a new idea for the fast and green synthesis of transition metal and carbon-based nanomaterials by microplasma.

A novel CoSe nanosphere@nitrogen-doped polymeric carbon dots are rapid prepared using ZIF precursor by simple dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma-induced on carbon cloth (CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC) acts as a self-supporting electrode has excellent sensing performance for dopamine.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: DA, as an important neurotransmitter in the human central nervous system, is closely related to some neurological diseases [1], so it is of great significance to develop an efficient method for rapid and accurate detection of DA the content. At present, some methods have been proposed for sensitive DA detection, such as capillary electrophoresis [2], fluorescence spectrometry [3], liquid chromatography [4] and so on, but their application were mostly limited by the factors of expensive instrument, time consuming and low stability [5]. Electrochemical detection methods can overcome these shortcomings and therefore show great potential in DA detection [6].

Zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF) is a promising electrocatalytic material due to their high surface area and high porosity [7]. The original ZIF materials are hindered by poor conductivity, so the preparation of transition metal and carbon-based complexes by ZIF derivation is gradually favored by researchers, such as sulfide [8], phosphating [9], selenide [10] and so on. Among them, transition metal selenides (TMSs) have attracted much attention due to their unusual chemical, physical or electronic properties [11], rich chemical states, low cost and structural properties [12], and are considered as promising materials for electrocatalytic applications. However, due to the disadvantages of poor stability and low ionic conductivity, the electrocatalytic activity of TMSs is insufficient.

Studies have shown that combining TMs with a variety of conductive carbonaceous materials [13,14], including carbon nanotubes, graphite, carbon dots [15] (CDs) and graphene can greatly improve the structural and mechanical stability [16] and increase its conductivity and specific surface area [17–19], which has been one of the effective strategies to improve the electrocatalytic activity of TMs. CDs, in particular, is widely used in the electrochemical sensing field because of its advantages of low cost, simple synthesis, significant conductivity, and excellent chemical stability. In recent years, various methods have been used to prepare CDs with different functions. Based different carbon sources, it is mainly divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up” two modes, including arc discharge method, electrochemical/chemical oxidation method, combustion method, microwave assisted method and hydrothermal/solvothermal method [20]. For instance, Wu et al. used candle ash as raw material to prepare CDs by nitric acid oxidation method, which showed highly sensitive electrochemical sensing performance for DA [21]. Jiang et al. constructed a DA electrochemical sensor using microwave assisted preparation of N-doped CDs [5]. Nevertheless, most of the reported preparation methods of TMSs/carbon-based composite nanomaterials require annealing at higher temperature for a long time [22], which does not meet the requirements of sustainable development. For example, Feng et al. combined hydrothermal reaction (180 °C 10 h) with anion exchange process (140 °C 12 h) to prepared 3D sea urchin-like CoSe arrays with nitrogen doped carbon dots on nickel foam (CoSe2/NCDs/NF) [23]. Bhattacharyya et al. synthesized a novel CdSe/carbon quantum dots by heating (225 °C) as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of tuberculosis biomarkers [24]. However, the green and economical preparation of TMSs/CDs complexes using ZIF derivatives is rarely reported. Therefore, it is meaningful to explore an energy-saving and rapid method to complete these complexes.

Dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma is a kind of plasma which has the advantages of small physical size, high electron density and can operate stably at room temperature and normal pressure. Due to the microplasma interacts with the liquid to produce solvated electrons (eaq −) and free radicals, it can produce chemical reactions at atmospheric pressure and low temperature to accelerate the generation of nanoparticles [25,26]. Herein, a CoSe nanospheres@nitrogen-doped polymeric carbon dots was fast prepared derived from ZIF on carbon cloth (CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC) using DBD microplasma at room temperature and pressure for the first time. CoSe nanospheres supported N-PCDs can effectively prevent their aggregation and increase their specific surface area, thus significantly improving the electrocatalytic activity of the composite. The constructed CoSe@NPCDs/CC electrode shows high sensitivity, stability and selectivity for DA detection under neutral condition, and has been successfully applied to the determination of real human serum samples. The theoretical DFT results show that the (101) crystal surface of CoSe can interact with DA most effectively and promote the electrocatalytic oxidation of DA.

Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 2-methylimidazole, SeO2, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaCl, urea, glucose (Glu), DA hydrochloride, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), hydrazine hydrate and ethyl alcohol were purchased from Kelong reagent Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). The carbon cloth (CC) used was purchased from Surgical Instruments company (Wuhan, China). Human serum samples were provided by West China Hospital, Sichuan Province (China). All reagents except the carbon cloth were analytically pure and used directly without further treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZIF-67 on carbon cloth (ZIF/CC)
ce:section.ce:para: CC was first treated in concentrated nitric acid at 90 °C for 6 h. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1.164 g) and 2-methylimidazole (2.627 g) were dissolved in 80 mL and 40 mL deionized water respectively to form solution A and solution B, and then the two solutions were mixed to form purple solution (solution C). A clean CC (1 × 3 cm2) was placed in solution C and kept at room temperature for 12 h. After the reaction ended, and ZIF/CC was washed alternately with ethanol and water for three times and dried overnight at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC by DBD microplasma
ce:section.ce:para: The DBD microplasma reactor details refer to our previous work [27]. Brifely, SeO2 (20 mg) and hydrazine hydrate (0.5 mL) were added to 5 mL deionized water to form solution D. Solution D and ZIF/CC precursor were added to the DBD microplasma reactor, and the reaction was carried out at different voltage (60 V, 70 V, 80 V, 90 V, and 100 V) for 20 min, 40 min, 60 min, 80 min, and 100 min. As depicted in Fig. S1 that the optimal synthesis time and voltage were 60 min and 80 V, respectively. Washing and drying procedures were the same as above. Then, the target catalyst CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC was obtained (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for preparing CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC by DBD microplasma
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, in order to study the effects of microplasma-induced time and voltage on the performance of CoSe@NPCDs/CC DA sensor, the optimization experiments were carried out. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) response curves of the microplasma-prepared electrode to 0.1 mM DA at different times (20 min, 40 min, 60 min, 80 min, and 100 min) and voltages (60 V, 70 V, 80 V, 90 V, and 100 V) are shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3, respectively. Theresults indicates that the optimal reaction time and voltage were 60 min and 80 V, this may be due to the fact that CoSe nanospheres began to accumulate after 60 min (Figs. S2a–S2e), which hindered electron transmission [28]. XRD images (Fig. S2f) confirms that the diffraction peaks of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC are consistent with JCPDS No. 04-003-7223 of CoSe at different reaction times. There is no significant difference in the morphology of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC prepared at different reaction voltages (Figs. S3a–S3e), but it is observed in XRD images that the catalyst has the best crystallinity at 80 V (Fig. S3f), which may be related to the best DA sensing performance [29,30].

As seen in XRD pattern, CC, ZIF/CC and CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC all display two diffraction peaks from CC (Fig. 2 a), and XRD diffraction peaks of ZIF/CC match well with previous studies [7,31] (Fig. S4). In Fig. 2a, the main diffraction peaks of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC centered at 33.2°, 33.9°, 44.8°, 50.4° and 66.7° are related to (101), (002), (102), (110) and (201) crystal planes of CoSe, respectively. The XRD diffraction peak (Fig. S5) of the powder scraped from the CC shows a wide peak at 24.5°, which may be related to the highly cross-linked polymer skeletons [31,32]. Fig. S6 displays SEM image of CC, indicating a clean surface of the CC. The SEM image of ZIF/CC (Fig. 2b) confirms that the flaky structure of ZIF-67 grows uniformly on CC, and the original morphology is completely destroyed after the treatment of the microplasma, forming a uniform spherical morphology composed of many nanospheres (Fig. 2c). The formation of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs may be attributed to radicals and solvated electrons (eaq −) from the interaction of the microplasma-liquid to destroy the ZIF-67 to form hydrocarbons and Co2+, and further condensation and selenization to form the target catalyst [26,33]. TEM image (Fig. 2d) further demonstrates the microsphere morphology of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs, and the diameter is about 159 nm (Fig. S7). Fig. 2e demonstrates the presence of N-PCDs (marked by red dotted circles). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs (Fig. 2f) disclosed the lattice spacing of 0.296 nm corresponding to the (101) lattice plane of the CoSe phase, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2g) illustrates the (101) plane of CoSe. However, no crystal lattices of the CDs are observed (Fig. 2f), which proves that the synthesized CDs belong to amorphous polymeric CDs [34,35]. High angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF - STEM) and corresponding elemental mapping images (Fig. 2h–m) display uniform distribution of C, Co, N, O and Se elements are evenly distributed on CoSe microspheres, which can effectively solve the problem of CDs aggregation and improve the conductivity.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed to further elucidate the state and chemical composition of CoSe@NPCDs/CC. In Fig. 3 a, full-scan XPS spectra uncovers the presence of C, N, O, Co, and Se elements in CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC, which is consistent with the results of mapping (Fig. 2h–m). The XPS spectrum of C 1s can be fitted into four bands, corresponding to C–C at 282.7 eV, abundant C–N/CN at 284.5 eV, C–O at 286.0 eV and CO at 287.5 eV (Fig. 3b) [36,37]. Fig. 3c shows the N 1s core-level spectrum of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC, the peaks located at 402.5 eV, 400.3 eV, and 398.8 eV are respectively attributed to the quaternary type N, pyrrolic type N and pyridinic type N [38], indicating the successful nitrogen doping of C [39]. Nitrogen doping improves electron transport and amplifies the electrochemical detection signal generated by DA oxidation [40,41]. The spectrum of Co 2p displayed in Fig. 2d has six obvious peaks, which contain two characteristic peaks assigned to Co2+ (located at 780.6 eV and 797.1 eV), the peaks related to Co3+ (778.7 eV and 793.8 eV) and two satellite peaks centered at 783.3 eV and 801.4 eV (indicated as “Sat.”) [42,43]. In the Se 3d spectrum (Fig. 3e), two peak at 55.3 eV and 54.5 eV are ascribed to Se2− of 3d3/2 and 3d5/2, respectively, and the peak located at around 59.4 eV is related to SeOx species by oxidation [44,45]. In adsorption-desorption curves and pore size distribution of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC (Fig. 3f), the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, pore volume and average pore width are 3.38 m2/g, 0.0095 cm3/g and 4.8 nm, respectively. The results show that the CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC has mesoporous structure and large specific surface area, thus enhancing the sensing performance [46].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was tested to investigate the presence of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC functional groups. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the peaks at 2924/2849 cm−1, 2360 cm−1, 1740 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, 1463 cm−1 and 1391 cm−1 are due to the symmetric/asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–H, stretching vibration of CO in CO2, stretching vibration of CO, CC stretching, C–N stretching vibration and bending vibration of the C–O, respectively [47–49], which is consistent with the results of XPS spectra (Fig. 3c) and confirm the existence of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of N-PCDs. Interestingly, the Raman spectrum of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC (Fig. 4b) do not appear obvious D bands and G bands, which is consistent with the characteristics of the polymer CDs [31,35]. In Fig. S8, the water contact angle value of ZIF/CC and CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC are 31.1° and 74.6°, respectively, which proves that the latter has better hydrophilicity than the former.

The prepared self-supported CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/ CC electrode was used as the working electrode (area: 0.04 cm−2, loading: 1.1 mg cm−2), the Pt wire and Ag/AgCl electrodes were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode for electrochemical testing in 0.1 M PBS(the physical photos are shown in Fig. S9). Fig. 5 a shows the CV plots before and after adding 1 mM DA to the PBS electrolyte solution with CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC, ZIF/CC and CC as working electrodes, respectively. The results show that the peak current density of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC increases by 16.01 mA cm−2 after adding 1 mM DA, which is significantly greater than that of CC (1.18 mA cm−2) and ZIF/CC (3.08 mA cm−2) precursors (inset in Fig. 5a), indicating that the catalytic oxidation activity of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC on DA is superior to that of ZIF/CC and CC. In addition, the oxidation peak current increases with the DA concentration from 0 to 0.4 mM (Fig. 5b), which further proves that the catalyst has remarkable catalytic performance for DA oxidation. As the pH value of electrolyte has a significant effect on the catalytic DA oxidation of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC, the relationship between oxidation peak current density (j pa) and oxidation peak potential (Epa) and pH value (6.2–7.8) was studied by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) (Fig. S10). As displayed in Fig. 5c, the j pa increases with the increase of pH value and reaches the maximum value when pH is 7.4. Therefore, 0.1 M PBS with pH equal to 7.4 is selected as the electrolyte, and the value is also equal to physiological value. In this condition, DA molecules exist as cations, and the carboxyl group on the surface of the N-PCDs is negatively charged, electrostatic attraction and the hydrogen bond between the phenol hydroxyl group of DA and the amino group on the surface of N-PCDs are beneficial to promote the oxidation of DA and improve the selectivity of detection [50,51]. At the same time, Epa presents a good linear correlation with pH value, and the linear regression curve is: Epa = −0.0591 pH + 0.615 (R2 = 0.998). According to the Nernst equation:
(1) Epa = E0 + (0.0591/n) • log[(ox)a/(R)b] – (0.0591 m/n) pH

(m and n represent the number of protons and electrons, respectively), the m/n of DA oxidation can be calculated as 1. In addition, the CV curves of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC at different scan rate (Fig. S11a) show that the oxidation peak potential (Epa) and reduced peak potential (Epc) have good linear relationship with the logarithmic of scan rate (Fig. S11b): Epa (V) = 0.083 lg ν (V s−1) + 0.336 (R2 = 0.993), Epc (V) = −0.063 lg ν (V s−1) + 0.065 (R2 = 0.988). According to the Laviron equation:

Here, F，R and T are 96,500 C mol−1，8.314 J mol−1 K−1 and 298K, respectively. The final calculated electron transfer coefficient (α) and the electron transfer number (n) are about 0.57 and 2, respectively. Above results prove that DA oxidation reaction is a two-electron/two-proton electrochemical system [52]. Therefore, the possibility mechanism of DA oxidation is as follows:
(4) DA - 2H+ - 2e− = DAQ

In Fig. 5d, the result demonstrates that both j pa and reduced peak current density (j pc) have good linear relationship with the square root of the scan rate (ν): j pa = 0.960ν + 0.6 (R2 = 0.998) and j pc = −1.076ν + 2.67 (R2 = 0.998), indicating that the entire catalytic process of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs for DA is a typical diffusion control process [53]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective method to study the solution resistance and charge transfer resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface [54], and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) can be calculated from the diameter of the semicircular. According to the equivalent circuit diagram fitting in Fig. S12, the Rcts of CC, ZIF/CC and CoSe@NPCDs/CC are 23.9 Ω, 27.3 Ω, 20.5 Ω, respectively, indicating that CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC has good conductivity and can effectively promote the electron transfer rate on the electrode surface. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) can be evaluated using double-layer capacitors (Cdl) by CV tests in non-Faraday regions (Figs. S13a–13c) [55]. According to the equations Δj = vCdl and ECSA = Cdl/Cs (Cs = 0.23 mF cm−2 in 0.1 M PBS solution) [56], the Cdl of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC, ZIF/CC and CC are 26.62, 20.55, 11.60 mF cm−2 (Fig. S13d), and corresponding ECSA can be calculated as 50.43, 89.35 and 115.74 (Fig. S13e), respectively, which proves that CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC has a larger ECSA to provide more catalytic active sites, thus enhancing catalytic activity [57].

DPV is adopted to investigate the electrochemical sensing performance of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC for DA (Fig. 6 a). In the range of DA concentration from 0.1 to 50 μM (the background current has been subtracted), the corresponding linear fitting equation is j pa (mA cm−2) = 80.7 cDA (mM) with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.997 (Fig. 6b). The sensitivity and detection limit (LOD) can be calculated as 80,734 μA cm−2 mM−1 and 40.2 nM, respectively. A kink can be observed at DA concentration of 0.05 mM, which may be due to the rapid oxidation of DA on the electrode surface by the high catalytic activity of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC catalyst at a low DA concentration, resulting in a high sensitivity (high slope) sensing performance. However, when the concentration of DA is large, the reaction products generated on the surface may block the pores of the catalyst, reduce the catalytic activity of the catalyst, resulting in surface passivation and lower sensitivity [58,59]. Compared with other metal and carbon-based DA sensors (Table 1 ), the electrode shows good DA sensing performance, especially LOD, which is lower than most reported results, including some precious metal based electrodes, due to the fact that CoSe nanospheres can effectively prevent the aggregation of N-PCDs. Nitrogen doping can tune its electronic structure, which significantly improves the electrocatalytic activity of the composite. Moreover, the DBD microplasma synthesis method of the electrode is simple, fast and green, and has potential practical application potential. Considering the possible interferences (including Glu, UA, AA, NaCl and Ure) with DA detection in human serum samples by the CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrode [26], interference test was performed(Fig. S14 and Fig. 6c). As shown in Fig. 6d, when different concentrations (1 mM, 2 mM, 3 mM and 4 mM) of interferences coexist, the j pa value changes are negligible, proving that the electrode has good selectivity, which is due to the electrostatic attraction between N-PCDs and DA [51,52].

To further understand the selectivity of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrode towards DA, density functional theory (DFT) calculations have been carried out to study the interaction mechanisms of DA and the possible interferences (Glu, AA and UA) with the CoSe (101) surface. As shown in Fig. 7 , it can be found that the Co2+ site acts as the active center, producing chemical adsorption capacity for DA and these interferences (including Glu, AA and UA). Consequently, the adsorption energies (E ad) were computed to be −1.71, −0.99, −1.20 and −1.29 eV for DA, Glu, AA and UA, respectively, indicating the strongest adsorption for DA other than the interferences (including Glu, AA and UA). The most effective interaction with DA on CoSe (101) surface can promote the electrochemical response and the catalytic DA oxidation. Hence, the calculated results have further explained the good selectivity of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrode for DA despite the coexistence of interferences.

Stability and reproducibility are also important indicators for evaluating analytical sensors. The same CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrode is used to test 0.1 mM DA every five days under the same conditions (Fig. 6e). It is found that the j pa of the sensor still maintains 86.63 % of the initial value after two months, which indicates that the CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC sensor has excellent stability. In the illustration of Fig. 6e, fifteen independently prepared electrodes are subjected to DPV testing in the presence of 0.1 mM DA, and the mean relative standard deviation (RSD) is 1.64 %, confirming the high reproducibility of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC as a DA sensing electrode.

In order to ensure the accuracy of this method in analyzing DA in real samples, the recovery rate of DA in human serum was determined by standard addition method under DPV mode. After ultrafiltration treatment, the obtained human serum samples were diluted 10 times with 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) to obtain biological samples. Then, three different known concentrations of DA solutions (5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM) are added into the diluted serum to obtain the spiked samples for electrochemical test, as shown in Fig. S15. The results in Table 2 show that the recoveries rate is 98.3%–106.5 % and RSD is between 0.39 % and 2.78 %, which proves that CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC sensing electrode has a potential application prospect in the detection of DA in practical samples.

In summary, the CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrochemical sensing electrode is fast in situ successfully synthesized using ZIF as the precursor by a simple, energy-saving DBD microplasma. On the one hand, nitrogen doping can produce a strong and tight interaction between metal and carbon. At the same time, CoSe NSs can promote rapid proton transfer and effectively prevent the aggregation of functional-rich N-PCDs, which significantly improves the electrocatalytic activity of the composite. The non-enzymatic catalyst has the advantages of super high sensitivity, low detection limit (40.2 nM), high repeatability, good selectivity and good stability for the detection of DA. This sensor has been successfully applied to the detection of DA level in human real serum samples. The mechanism of high selectivity of CoSe NSs@N-PCDs/CC electrode for DA is investigated by DFT theoretical calculation. The results show that CoSe (101) surface has the most effective interaction with DA. This work provides a new idea for simple and rapid synthesis of transition metal selenide/carbon nanomaterial composite sensing electrode materials by DBD microplasma. Although there are still many uncertainties in this method, such as the DBD microplasma synthesis mechanism needs to be further explored, by adjusting the synthesis conditions of DBD, the structure, composition and morphology of MOF derivatives, we believe that this strategy can open a new door for the preparation of TMs/carbon-based complexes and improve the electrochemical sensing performance.
